Title: Web Application SQL Injection Attack
Description: SQL injection attack against OmniPortal web application exploiting unsanitized input fields in authentication form
Initial_Response: Isolated affected application; captured evidence; began forensic analysis
Root_Cause: Missing input validation on login form parameters; no parameterized queries
Impact: Potential access to user database records; authentication bypass demonstrated
Remediation_Actions: Implemented parameterized queries; added WAF rules; conducted code review
Lessons_Learned: Always test for injection vulnerabilities during development phase; parameterized queries are non-negotiable
Framework_Reference: NIST IR-4; ISO A.5.26; SOC CC7.4

Title: Brute Force Attack on Linux SSH
Description: Automated brute force attack detected against SSH service on Linux lab system; 847 failed login attempts in 10 minutes
Initial_Response: Account temporarily locked; IP blocked via UFW; alert triggered
Root_Cause: Externally exposed SSH without rate limiting; no IP allowlist
Impact: No successful authentication; service temporarily degraded
Remediation_Actions: Implemented fail2ban; UFW rate limiting; SSH key-only authentication
Lessons_Learned: Deploy brute force detection before exposing any authentication service to network; log analysis is critical
Framework_Reference: NIST AC-7; ISO A.8.16; SOC CC7.2

Title: XSS Session Cookie Theft Attempt
Description: Reflected XSS attack against OmniPortal exploiting unencoded user input in search functionality; session cookie targeted
Initial_Response: Isolated affected endpoint; captured XSS payload; reviewed session management
Root_Cause: No output encoding on search results; session cookies lacked HttpOnly flag
Impact: Session cookie exposure demonstrated; potential account takeover
Remediation_Actions: Implemented output encoding; added HttpOnly and Secure flags; added CSP header
Lessons_Learned: Output encoding must be applied at every output point; CSP is a critical defense-in-depth layer
Framework_Reference: NIST SI-10; ISO A.8.25; SOC CC7.3

Title: Unauthorized Network Reconnaissance
Description: Nmap scanning detected against lab network from external address; systematic port scan of all services
Initial_Response: Logged scan details; blocked source IP; reviewed exposed services
Root_Cause: Insufficient alerting for port scan patterns; some services unnecessarily exposed
Impact: Attack surface mapped by attacker; no data compromised
Remediation_Actions: Added Suricata rule for SYN scan detection; closed unnecessary open ports
Lessons_Learned: Proactive scanning of own assets before attackers is essential; IDS rules must cover recon patterns
Framework_Reference: NIST SI-4; ISO A.8.16; SOC CC7.1

Title: Privilege Escalation via Misconfigured Sudo
Description: Low-privilege user account gained root access via misconfigured sudo rules allowing unrestricted command execution
Initial_Response: Revoked elevated privileges; audited all sudo rules; reviewed access logs
Root_Cause: Overly permissive sudo rule (ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL) left from initial setup
Impact: Temporary root access by low-privilege account; no data exfiltration detected
Remediation_Actions: Corrected sudo rules to minimum required; implemented sudo audit logging; added sudoers review to harden.sh
Lessons_Learned: Never use wildcard sudo rules; principle of least privilege applies to sudo configuration
Framework_Reference: NIST AC-6; ISO A.8.2; SOC CC6.3

Title: Malware Persistence via Scheduled Task
Description: Simulated malware persistence mechanism detected - malicious scheduled task created by post-exploitation module during purple team exercise
Initial_Response: Isolated system from network; captured memory image; removed persistence mechanism
Root_Cause: Post-exploitation module created scheduled task for persistence; insufficient task monitoring
Impact: Persistence mechanism established in lab environment; no production impact
Remediation_Actions: Removed malicious task; implemented task scheduler auditing; updated EDR signatures
Lessons_Learned: Scheduled task creation should be monitored and alerted; memory forensics is essential for persistence detection
Framework_Reference: NIST IR-4; ISO A.5.26; SOC CC7.4

Title: Active Directory Kerberoasting Attempt
Description: Kerberoasting attack detected during purple team exercise - attacker requested service tickets for offline cracking
Initial_Response: Reset service account passwords; implemented LAPS; reviewed SPN registrations
Root_Cause: Service accounts with weak passwords and SPNs registered; insufficient Kerberos monitoring
Impact: Service ticket captured for offline analysis; no credential compromise confirmed
Remediation_Actions: Implemented 25+ character service account passwords; deployed LAPS; added Kerberos anomaly detection
Lessons_Learned: Service accounts are high-value targets; managed service accounts and LAPS significantly reduce Kerberoasting risk
Framework_Reference: NIST IA-5; ISO A.8.5; SOC CC6.2

Title: Data Exfiltration via DNS Tunneling
Description: Simulated DNS tunneling detected during final capstone exercise - sensitive data encoded in DNS queries to external resolver
Detection_Method: Suricata IDS - custom DNS detection rule
Initial_Response: Blocked external DNS; implemented DNS sinkholing; captured traffic for analysis
Root_Cause: No DNS query inspection; external DNS resolution allowed for all hosts
Impact: Simulated data exfiltration demonstrated; no real data at risk in lab environment
Remediation_Actions: Implemented internal DNS resolver; blocked external DNS; added DNS anomaly detection to Suricata
Lessons_Learned: DNS tunneling is a powerful exfiltration technique; DNS inspection and internal resolvers are essential controls
Framework_Reference: NIST SC-7; ISO A.8.20; SOC CC6.6

Title: Forensic Investigation - Apache Log Tampering
Description: During DFIR exercise attacker was found to have modified Apache access logs to remove evidence of compromise
Initial_Response: Recovered original logs from memory forensics; documented chain of custody; preserved all evidence
Root_Cause: No log integrity protection; logs stored on same system as application
Impact: Log tampering obscured attack timeline; recovered via memory forensics
Remediation_Actions: Implemented remote syslog; added log integrity monitoring; updated chain-of-custody procedures
Lessons_Learned: Remote syslog is essential - logs stored on compromised systems cannot be trusted; memory forensics can recover deleted/modified logs
Framework_Reference: NIST AU-9; ISO A.8.15; SOC CC7.3

Title: Container Breakout Attempt
Description: Attempted container breakout via mounted Docker socket exploitation during penetration testing exercise
Initial_Response: Removed Docker socket mount; audited all container configurations; updated docker-compose.yml
Root_Cause: Docker socket mounted into container allowing host-level command execution
Impact: Container isolation compromised in test; corrected immediately
Remediation_Actions: Removed all unnecessary volume mounts; implemented container security policies; added runtime protection
Lessons_Learned: Never mount Docker socket into containers; treat container breakout as critical risk
Framework_Reference: NIST CM-7; ISO A.8.9; SOC CC6.8
